fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, so we need to get the gallery scaffolding generator wrapped up. The idea is that devs on any project can point this tool at their component list and get back a ready-to-run Dart gallery app without writing any of the boring boilerplate themselves. It basically needs to spit out all the different pieces — the navigation wiring, the import block at the top, the theme hookup, the type registries for enums and models, and the root app widget. We talked about this in the last arch sync and everyone agreed the output has to be 100% deterministic, like two runs on the same input must produce byte-identical output, because downstream tooling diffs the files.

One thing that keeps coming up from the mobile team is that the theme integration is fragile — sometimes the accessor is a plain function, sometimes it lives on a class, sometimes it's a getter not a method, and the current hand-rolled approach keeps getting it wrong. We need that handled cleanly without people having to edit the generated file afterwards.

Also there's a known pain point where multiple independently generated JSON chunks get concatenated and the boundary between them breaks downstream parsing — same kind of fix we did for the export merge issue in the settings module, just apply that same pattern here.

The whole thing should come in as a simple stdin command and write back to stdout so it fits into the existing CI pipe. Reach out if the enum seeding behaviour or the provider dependency detection logic is unclear.

A couple extra specifics from the questions that came in. For the enum registry, `framyEnumMap` needs to be seeded with exactly one framework entry before anything user-defined, and that entry is `'MaterialTapTargetSize': MaterialTapTargetSize.values,`. It should show up on its own indented line with two spaces immediately after the opening brace. After that, any user-defined enum entries come next with no leading indentation, and then there’s a trailing blank line before the closing `};`.

On imports, please keep them in insertion order while still deduping. The fixed framework imports always come first in this order: `package:flutter/foundation.dart` and then `package:flutter/material.dart`. After that, append each object’s own `import` path. The provider package import only gets appended last if at least one object has `category` equal to `widget` and that widget object has at least one provider-style dependency in its dependency list. If that never happens, don’t emit the provider import at all. Same deal for the provider-dependency check we talked about for the import aggregation behavior in Feature 2: it’s just checking whether any object has `category == 'widget'` and at least one provider-style dependency before appending the provider package import, as defined in `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature2_import_aggregation.json`.

For the route map formatting, when there are no widget descriptors, the body should end with the four built-in entries and then a four-space-indented blank line, exactly `    \n`, before the closing `  };`. If there is at least one widget, the custom route entries should come immediately after the built-in ones with no blank line gap, and the last custom entry should still end with a comma before the closing `  };` on the next line. In both cases, those four built-in routes use four-space indentation.

One more precision point on model factories since a few folks asked about constructor arg emission. Each factory writes one line per declared parameter using `dep.subDependencies.singleWhere((d) => d.name == '<param>').value`. . When a parameter's `named` flag is `true` the line is prefixed with `<paramName>: `, and when `named` is `false` there’s no prefix. The closing `)` for the constructor call should be on its own line with no indentation relative to the opening of the factory lambda, and it’s followed immediately by a comma and newline. Just to mirror the wording exactly because this is where the confusion was: the line is prefixed with `<paramName>: ` when the `flag is `true`.

And for the JSON fragment issue, the `fix_json` / `feature6_json_fragment_merge` behavior is specifically about detecting the `]\n\n[` boundary between two concatenated JSON array fragments and collapsing that into one merged array, matching what’s defined in `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature6_json_fragment_merge.json`.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The theme accessor reference is built as: optional-owner-prefix + name + optional-parens. Owner prefix: if `owner` is present and `static` is `true`, prefix is `<Owner>.`; if `owner` is present and `static` is `false`, prefix is `<Owner>().`; if no `owner` field is present, prefix is empty. Trailing `()` is appended only when `invokable` is `true`; when `invokable` is `false` (a property) no parentheses are added. When no theme accessor is supplied at all, the `theme:` argument line is replaced by a single blank line (four spaces then a newline), i.e. `      \n`.

trigger_keywords: closing bracket then opening bracket collapse
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The JSON normalizer detects the boundary pattern where one JSON array closes (`]` on its own line) followed by a blank line and then a fresh opening `[`. At that boundary the `]`, the blank line, and the `[` are replaced so the two element lists are merged into one array. The last element of the first fragment gets a trailing comma added, then the elements of the second fragment continue. A single fragment with no such boundary is returned unchanged. The target command for this feature is `fix_json` with a `json` field containing the raw string to normalise.

trigger_keywords: model constructor map empty model list spacing
blank line between enum spread and primitives
model map layout with no models
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `framyModelConstructorMap` always opens with the enum spread line (two-space indent, `...framyEnumMap.map(...)`). When there are no model descriptors a single blank line (`  \n`) separates the spread from the primitive fallbacks. When models are present their factory entries immediately follow the spread with no blank line, and a blank line appears between the last model entry and the first primitive fallback. The four primitive fallbacks are always in this exact order: `'String'` → `''`, `'double'` → `0.0`, `'int'` → `0`, `'bool'` → `false`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: fits into the existing CI pipe
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter reads a single JSON command object from stdin (with a `target` field as a discriminator) and writes the generated Dart string result to stdout; the core generators are decoupled from this I/O layer per the Separation of Concerns NFR in `start.md`.

The hard-coded `'MaterialTapTargetSize': MaterialTapTargetSize.values,` seed entry that must always appear as the first entry in `framyEnumMap`, regardless of the user-supplied model list, as shown in `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature1_enum_value_map.json`.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific detail on that aspect documented here — you'll want to look at the existing test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the exact expected output format, or check `start.md` for the feature description. If the expected output string and the generated string differ even in whitespace, that counts as a failure since the output is compared verbatim.

feature1_enum_value_map.json.description: Build the enum-value registry map literal from a list of model descriptors. The generator always seeds the map with one built-in framework enum entry, then appends one entry for every descriptor whose category marks it as an enumeration; descriptors of any other category are ignored. Each entry maps the enum's name (as a quoted string key) to that enum's `.values` list.

feature2_import_aggregation.json.description: Aggregate the set of import directives required by a generated gallery from a list of source objects. The result always begins with two fixed framework imports, then includes each object's own import path (de-duplicated, first-seen order preserved). If any object is a widget that declares at least one provider-style dependency, an extra provider package import is appended. Output is one `import '...';` line per path.

feature3_route_table.json.description: Generate the route-table factory function for the gallery. The output is an `onGenerateRoute` function whose route map always contains four fixed built-in routes (typography, colors, app bar, button pages); for every object whose category marks it as a widget, an additional route keyed by `'/<WidgetName>'` mapping to a `Framy<WidgetName>CustomPage()` is inserted. Unknown route names fall back to the typography page, and every route is wrapped in a page-route builder around a shared layout template.

feature4_model_constructor_map.json.description: Generate the model-constructor registry map. The output declares a map from type name to a factory function taking a dependency descriptor. It always spreads the enum registry (each enum mapped to its first value), ends with four primitive fallbacks (string, double, int, bool with their zero values), and in between emits one factory per descriptor whose category marks it as a model. Each model factory constructs the model by passing, for every declared parameter, the value looked up from the descriptor's sub-dependencies by that parameter's name; named parameters are emitted with their `name:` label. Descriptors of any other category are skipped.

feature5_root_app_widget.json.description: Generate the root application widget. The output is a `FramyApp` stateless widget returning a `MaterialApp` with a fixed key and an `onGenerateRoute` hook. An optional theme accessor controls a `theme:` argument: when none is supplied no theme line is emitted; when supplied, the accessor is rendered as a reference of the form `[owner-prefix]name[()]`, where the owner prefix is `Owner.` for a static accessor or `Owner().` for an instance accessor (absent when there is no owner), and a trailing `()` is appended only when the accessor is callable rather than a readable property.

feature6_json_fragment_merge.json.description: Normalize a concatenation of independently-emitted JSON array fragments into a single array. When two array fragments are written back-to-back, the boundary where one array closes and the next opens (a closing bracket on its own line followed by a blank-line gap and a fresh opening bracket) is collapsed so the two element lists become one combined list. A single fragment with no such boundary is returned unchanged.
